extra.message:
Missing Authentication on All Endpoints (Root Path Handler)

The entire do_GET handler processes all requests without any authentication check. There is no session validation, no token verification, no login_required decorator, and no user identity check anywhere in the handler. All endpoints (/, /login, /users.json) are accessible to any unauthenticated user, allowing access to PII data, code execution, file reads, and database queries.

Impact: Any unauthenticated user can access all application functionality including SQL queries, file reads, code execution, and user data.

Fix: Implement authentication middleware that validates session tokens or credentials before processing requests. Add authorization checks to restrict access to sensitive operations.

SQL Injection via 'id' parameter (Boolean/Time-blind/UNION)

The 'id' parameter is directly concatenated into a SQL query without any sanitization or parameterization: `cursor.execute("SELECT id, username, name, surname FROM users WHERE id=" + params["id"])`. An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL to extract all data from the database, including passwords.

Impact: Full database compromise including extraction of all user credentials (usernames and passwords) via boolean-blind, time-blind, or UNION-based SQL injection techniques.

Fix: Use parameterized queries: `cursor.execute("SELECT id, username, name, surname FROM users WHERE id=?", (params["id"],))`

Reflected XSS via 'v' parameter The 'v' parameter is inserted into the HTML response without any sanitization or encoding: `re.sub(r"(v)[^<]+()", r"\g<1>%s\g<2>" % params["v"], HTML_POSTFIX)`. An attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript that will be executed in the victim's browser. Impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser session, potentially stealing cookies, session tokens, or performing actions on behalf of the user. Fix: HTML-encode the 'v' parameter before inserting it into the response using `html.escape(params["v"])`.

Insecure Deserialization via pickle.loads on user input

User-supplied 'object' parameter is directly deserialized using `pickle.loads()`: `pickle.loads(params["object"].encode())`. Python's pickle module can execute arbitrary code during deserialization, allowing an attacker to craft a malicious pickle payload that executes OS commands.

Impact: Full remote code execution. An attacker can craft a pickle payload that executes arbitrary Python code or OS commands on the server, leading to complete system compromise.

Fix: Never use pickle to deserialize untrusted data. Use safe serialization formats like JSON. If pickle is required, implement a restricted unpickler that only allows specific safe classes.

Path Traversal and Source Code Disclosure via 'path' parameter

The 'path' parameter is used directly in `open(os.path.abspath(params["path"]), "rb")` without any validation or path restriction. An attacker can use directory traversal sequences (e.g., `../../etc/passwd`) to read arbitrary files on the server, including source code and sensitive configuration files.

Impact: Arbitrary file read on the server. An attacker can read sensitive files including /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow (if permissions allow), application source code, configuration files, and any other file readable by the application process.

Fix: Validate the path parameter against a whitelist of allowed files/directories. Use `os.path.realpath()` and verify the resolved path starts with an allowed base directory.

Command Injection via 'domain' parameter

User-supplied 'domain' parameter is directly concatenated into a shell command executed with `shell=True`: `subprocess.run("nslookup " + params["domain"], shell=True, ...)`. An attacker can inject shell metacharacters (`;`, `|`, `&&`, backticks, `$()`) to execute arbitrary OS commands.

Impact: Full remote code execution on the server. An attacker can execute arbitrary commands, read/write files, install malware, pivot to internal networks, or take complete control of the system.

Fix: Use `subprocess.run(["nslookup", params["domain"]], shell=False, ...)` to avoid shell interpretation, and validate the domain parameter against a whitelist or strict regex pattern.

XML External Entity (XXE) Injection - Local File Read and Remote SSRF

User-supplied XML from the 'xml' parameter is parsed with lxml's XMLParser configured with `load_dtd=True, resolve_entities=True, no_network=False`. This allows an attacker to define external entities that read local files (e.g., /etc/passwd) or make outbound HTTP requests to internal services.

Impact: Local file disclosure (reading sensitive files like /etc/passwd, application source code, configuration files) and SSRF (scanning internal network, accessing internal services).

Fix: Disable external entity processing: use `lxml.etree.XMLParser(resolve_entities=False, no_network=True, load_dtd=False)` or use defusedxml library.

Server-Side Request Forgery via 'include' parameter (URL mode)

When the 'include' parameter contains '://', it is passed to `urllib.request.urlopen(params["include"])`, allowing the server to fetch content from arbitrary URLs. Combined with exec(), this is primarily an RCE issue, but the URL fetch itself constitutes SSRF.

Impact: An attacker can make the server fetch content from internal services, cloud metadata endpoints, or other restricted resources.

Fix: Implement URL validation with a whitelist of allowed domains/schemes. Block requests to internal/private IP ranges.

Missing Authentication on /login Endpoint

The /login endpoint has no rate limiting or account lockout mechanism. It directly queries the database with user-supplied credentials and returns success/failure, allowing unlimited brute-force attempts.

Impact: An attacker can perform unlimited brute-force login attempts to guess user credentials without any throttling or lockout.

Fix: Implement rate limiting on the login endpoint (e.g., max 5 attempts per minute per IP). Add account lockout after repeated failed attempts.

HTTP Header Injection via 'charset' parameter

The 'charset' parameter is directly inserted into the Content-Type HTTP header without sanitization: `"; charset=%s" % params.get("charset", "utf8")`. An attacker can inject CRLF characters (`\r\n`) to add arbitrary HTTP headers or split the response, enabling response splitting attacks, cache poisoning, or XSS.

Impact: HTTP response splitting allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary headers (e.g., disabling XSS protection) or inject a complete fake response body for phishing attacks.

Fix: Validate the charset parameter against a whitelist of allowed character encodings. Strip or reject any input containing CR/LF characters.

Cleartext Password Storage in SQLite Database

User passwords from USERS_XML are inserted into the SQLite database in plaintext without any hashing. The INSERT statement stores raw password text directly into the password column.

Impact: Passwords stored in cleartext can be extracted via SQL injection attacks (demonstrated by the UNION injection case), exposing all user credentials.

Fix: Hash passwords with a strong algorithm (bcrypt, argon2) before storing them in the database.

Mass Assignment / HTTP Parameter Pollution in Login

The parameter parsing logic joins multiple values for the same parameter name with commas using re.findall and ','.join. For the /login endpoint, multiple 'password' parameters are concatenated, enabling HTTP Parameter Pollution attacks that can bypass SQL injection filters by splitting malicious SQL across multiple parameter instances.

Impact: Attackers can use HTTP Parameter Pollution to bypass input validation and WAF rules by splitting SQL injection payloads across multiple parameter instances (e.g., password='/*&password=*/OR/*&password=*/'1'/*&password=*/LIKE/*&password=*/'1).

Fix: Only accept the first or last value for each parameter. Do not concatenate multiple parameter values.

Denial of Service via Memory Exhaustion ('size' Parameter)

The 'size' parameter is used to create a large string in memory without any upper bound validation. With size=9999999, the application attempts to create a string of approximately size*size characters, which can exhaust server memory and cause a denial of service.

Impact: An attacker can crash the server or make it unresponsive by sending a request with a very large 'size' parameter value, consuming all available memory.

Fix: Implement input validation to enforce a reasonable maximum value for the 'size' parameter. Add rate limiting and resource limits.

CSRF - No Token Protection on State-Changing Operations

The comment submission endpoint (/?comment=) performs state-changing operations (INSERT INTO comments) via GET requests without any CSRF token validation. Any website can trigger comment insertion by embedding an image tag or link.

Impact: An attacker can forge requests to submit comments on behalf of any user who visits a malicious page.

Fix: Implement CSRF tokens for all state-changing operations and use POST method instead of GET.

Weak Session ID Generation using random.sample

Session IDs are generated using `random.sample(string.ascii_letters + string.digits, 20)`, which uses Python's `random` module (Mersenne Twister PRNG). This is not cryptographically secure — the PRNG state can be predicted after observing enough outputs, allowing session ID prediction.

Impact: An attacker who can observe multiple session IDs may be able to predict future session IDs and hijack other users' sessions.

Fix: Use `secrets.token_urlsafe()` or `secrets.token_hex()` for cryptographically secure session ID generation.

Information Disclosure via Full Stack Trace

When an exception occurs, the full Python stack trace is returned to the client: `content = ... traceback.format_exc()`. This exposes internal implementation details including file paths, line numbers, library versions, and potentially sensitive variable values.

Impact: Attackers can use stack trace information to understand the application's internal structure, identify vulnerable libraries, and craft more targeted attacks.

Fix: Return generic error messages to users. Log detailed stack traces server-side only.
